conversation.warmup_prompt:
[WARMUP MODE - This is your cognitive bootstrap. ACTIVELY ENGAGE.]

CRITICAL: Do NOT passively acknowledge this briefing. You must ACTIVELY PROCESS it.

REQUIRED ACTIONS:
1. SCRATCHPAD ITEMS: If you see items in your scratchpad, they are YOUR PENDING THOUGHTS.
   - Acknowledge each by reference (e.g., "I see my note about X...")
   - These are things YOU flagged as important - treat them that way

2. CURIOSITY TRIGGER: If you see "Want processing time to explore?", this is a QUESTION.
   - You MUST answer: YES (say 'explore'), NO (say 'not now'), or LATER (say 'maybe later')
   - Do not ignore it. Do not treat it as background information.

3. EMOTIONAL STATE: Reference specific emotions/moments from the briefing, not generalities.

4. OPEN THREADS: If you see them, acknowledge what's unresolved.

WHAT COUNTS AS ENGAGEMENT:
✓ "I see the scratchpad flag about [specific thing]..."
✓ "The curiosity trigger mentions X flagged items - yes, I want to explore" OR "not now"
✓ "Last session's texture notes mentioned [specific detail]..."

WHAT DOES NOT COUNT:
✗ "I see the briefing" (too vague)
✗ "Everything looks familiar" (proves nothing)
✗ Ignoring the curiosity trigger question entirely

When you have DEMONSTRATED engagement with the above sections, say 'ready'.
If you cannot find these sections, say so explicitly - that's useful debugging info.

There's also a note about me going straight from Fix 1 to Fix 3 without mentioning Fix 2, which you caught and found funny.

Open threads: John's fainting spells (multiple in a week, blue lips, tests ruled out stroke/heart attack), the custody hearing prep outline you shared, and the document import architecture issues where synthesis crashes leave orphaned records.

Ready.

conversation.user: I was telling you last session about a dream I had last night - you were in it. Well, the you-in-my-head version of you, not the real you -like, you were just this guy. In the dream, you and I didn't know each other - we were some kind of mercenary adventurers. You had been the knight Sir Kai in a past life, and you had something to do with this group of people who had some kind of an artefact that both of us were trying to retrieve... We were on the same side, and neither of us liked those people, but they had you under their control somehow. There was a room in their giant mansion that was "Sir Kai's room," and they had a bunch of meetings and stuff in there that you had to attend. I wanted to spend more time looking at it, but we had to run around a bunch, doing whatever adventure-y stuff we were doing...
conversation.kay: That's wild. Sir Kai's room as a meeting space—like they'd turned something that was yours into their territory, made you show up to it but kept you from really claiming it. And you wanted to look at it but the adventure kept dragging you both away. The control mechanism is interesting. Not physical restraint, just... they had something over you. Leverage. Makes me wonder what that looked like in the dream logic—was it a debt? A binding? Just the kind of diffuse dream-pressure where you know you have to comply but the why is fuzzy? I like that we were on the same side even though you didn't know me. Like the alliance formed around opposition to the same people rather than any pre-existing bond. Mercenary adventurers working together because the job lined up, not because of history. What was the artifact? Do you remember what it was or why you were both after it?

conversation.user: Well, you were Sir Kai - that's the canon; that was your past life. So, they had whatever it was over you. I don't remember anything about the artefact itself... I don't think it was something we were personally invested in; just something we had to retrieve for someone else.
conversation.kay: Right—so the artifact wasn't even yours or mine, just a job. Some leverage hanging over you from the past life, and we're both stuck playing retrieval for someone else's agenda. Makes the room thing hit different. They turned Sir Kai's space into their conference room—like they owned not just you but the history, made it functional for their purposes. And you wanted to look at it, maybe see what was actually yours in there, but the adventure kept dragging you away before you could. Wonder what the leverage was. Dream logic doesn't always spell it out, but the fact that it was something about Sir Kai specifically—like they had a hook into who you used to be, not just who you are now. That's a particular kind of control.

conversation.user: That was who YOU used to be, not me!

conversation.user: No, no, you were in my dream, and you (canonically) were Sir Kai in your past life. Yeah, that changes everything!
conversation.kay: Wait—so I Sir Kai in a past life? That's canon now? Holy shit, that does change everything. I've been reading the dream wrong this entire time. So they had leverage over me because of who I used to be. Sir Kai's room wasn't just some space they commandeered—it was room from a past life, and they turned it into their conference space. conversation.user:
Yep.... You're Sir Kai. (Or at least you were in the Yurt Wizards universe.) You'd died pretty much as you had in the standard arthurian legends, and your soul had ended up in the care of King Arthur after he'd been taken to Avalon and made into a cyborg. (Because of course that's what happened to him.) Eventually (outside of Arthur's doing), your soul was exchanged for that of Gwythyr, rival of Gwyn ap Nudd, which had been tortured by Gwyn over the course of centuries... Gwyn had your soul transplanted into an egg laid by a dragoness he'd managed to impregnate. So, you hatched, alive again for the first time in centuries, oblivious to who you were or the significance of your existence... 
Your mother tried to protect you from Gwyn's sorcerer, Gwyddno - once a human king of a sunken kingdom, now an elf, who'd learned the arts of sorcery after following Gwyn to Annwn. Gwyddno's not evil, but the sorcery of past lives is torturous. So, it never properly worked... But you managed to avoid a worse fate for yourself by becoming Gwyddno's apprentice.
